instructions: Eight questions across the module — the hierarchy, primary vs. secondary, binding vs. persuasive, and statute vs. common law. Answer each; the feedback explains every option. Your score is collected locally for the practice page’s submission file.

questions.prompt: An Arizona statute and a validly issued Arizona regulation on the same subject point in different directions. Which controls?

options.text: The statute — a regulation draws its force from the statute and yields to it
The regulation — it is more recent and more specific
Whichever one the agency says controls
Neither — a court must break the tie case by case

feedback.correct: A regulation exists only because a statute empowered the agency to issue it — so when the two conflict, the enacted statute outranks the regulation it authorized.
feedback.b: Recency and specificity matter within a level of the hierarchy, not across levels — the regulation still draws its force from the statute above it.
feedback.c: The agency cannot rank its own rule above the legislature’s enactment; its power comes from that enactment.
feedback.d: There is no tie to break — the hierarchy already decides it: statute over regulation.

questions.prompt: A statute is clear, but a litigant argues it violates the state constitution — and the state’s highest court agrees. Which authority wins?

options.text: The statute — the legislature speaks for the people
The constitution — it sits above every statute in the hierarchy
The court’s opinion — courts outrank legislatures
They are equal, so the more recent one governs

feedback.correct: The constitution tops the hierarchy; a statute that violates it falls. The court’s opinion is the instrument — the constitution is the authority doing the work.
feedback.a: The legislature’s power itself comes from the constitution — it cannot enact past the document that created it.
feedback.c: The court does not outrank the legislature; it applied a higher law. Strike the constitutional argument and the statute would have stood.
feedback.d: Constitutional and statutory law are not equals — the whole point of the hierarchy is that one creates and limits the other.

questions.prompt: Which of these is primary authority?

options.text: A treatise’s explanation of negligence doctrine
An ALR annotation collecting cases from forty states
A municipal ordinance regulating short-term rentals
A law review article proposing a new liability rule

feedback.correct: An ordinance is enacted law — the law itself, however local. Everything else on this list talks about the law: that is the whole distinction.
feedback.a: A treatise explains the law expertly — and is still secondary: about the law, never the law.
feedback.b: An ALR annotation collects and summarizes primary authority; the collection itself is secondary.
feedback.d: Scholarship analyzes and proposes — persuasive to read, secondary by definition.

questions.prompt: You found the perfect explanation of your rule in a legal encyclopedia. What belongs in your brief?

options.text: A citation to the encyclopedia — it states the rule cleanly
The primary authority its footnotes led you to, after you read it
Both, side by side, for extra support
Neither — encyclopedias are unreliable

feedback.correct: The encyclopedia’s job was to hand you the citations. You follow them, read the primary authority, confirm it is still good law, and cite that.
feedback.a: With rare exceptions you do not cite secondary sources as the law — the encyclopedia is the map, not the territory.
feedback.c: Adding the encyclopedia next to on-point primary authority adds weakness, not weight.
feedback.d: Encyclopedias are reliable at their actual job — orientation and citations — just not as citable authority.

questions.prompt: Before an Arizona trial court: a California Supreme Court decision squarely on point. What is it?

options.text: Binding — it is a state supreme court
Binding — it is squarely on point
Persuasive only — another jurisdiction’s law never binds an Arizona court
Nothing — out-of-state cases cannot even be cited

feedback.correct: Precedent runs down a single court system. California’s high court sits atop California’s system — in Arizona it can persuade, especially where Arizona law is unsettled, but it cannot bind.
feedback.a: Height in the wrong system is still the wrong system — bindingness follows the forum’s own ladder.
feedback.b: On-point makes it useful; it cannot make it binding across a jurisdictional line.
feedback.d: Too far — persuasive authority is citable and often valuable; it just is not controlling.

questions.prompt: The same published Ninth Circuit opinion is cited in two courts. Where does it bind?

options.text: In every federal court, because it is a federal appellate decision
In a U.S. District Court within the Ninth Circuit — and only persuades a district court in the Second
Nowhere — only the Supreme Court binds
In both, if the reasoning is strong enough

feedback.correct: Exactly the both-and of authority: one case, binding inside its own circuit’s ladder, persuasive outside it. The forum decides which face you see.
feedback.a: Circuits bind their own district courts — sister circuits get persuasion, not obedience.
feedback.c: The Supreme Court binds everywhere, but each circuit binds within its own system too.
feedback.d: Strong reasoning increases persuasive force; it never converts persuasion into obligation.

questions.prompt: For decades, a state’s courts applied a common-law rule. The legislature then enacts a statute squarely addressing the question with a different rule. Going forward?

options.text: The common-law rule — it is older and more developed
The statute — enacted law displaces the common law it addresses
Whichever a trial judge prefers
The common law, unless the statute says it overrides it

feedback.correct: Where a valid statute speaks to the question, it displaces the judge-made rule — the legislature can answer the courts back. The common law then governs only what the statute leaves open.
feedback.a: Age and development do not protect a common-law rule from a legislature that has spoken.
feedback.c: Judges apply the governing source; they do not choose between them by preference.
feedback.d: No magic words needed — a statute squarely addressing the question does the displacing by existing.

questions.prompt: On a question where the legislature has never enacted anything at all, where does the governing rule come from?

options.text: There is no law until the legislature acts
The common law — the rules courts have developed case by case
Federal law fills every state-law gap
The restatement of the majority rule

feedback.correct: Where statutes are silent, the common law governs — judge-made rules built decision by decision. That is why case law research never stops mattering.
feedback.a: Courts have been resolving disputes — and making law doing it — for centuries of legislative silence.
feedback.c: Federal law governs federal questions; it does not backfill state common-law subjects.
feedback.d: A Restatement describes the common law persuasively — the governing rule is the forum’s own case law.
